nativeRange.habitat: Seasonal wetlands, rice paddies, and shallow river margins across tropical West Africa

decorPreferences: midground to background placement with vertical room to grow
high lighting (PAR ~30-50) to develop copper-red coloration

summary: Ammannia senegalensis, sold as Red Ammannia or Copper Leaf Ammannia, is a demanding stem plant from tropical West Africa valued for its pink-orange to brick-red foliage. It is generally considered best suited to high-tech planted tanks with strong light, CO2, and iron-rich fertilization.
careNotes: Ammannia senegalensis is a stem plant native to tropical West Africa, where it commonly grows in seasonal wetlands, rice paddies, and shallow river margins — particularly across Senegal and Mali. In the aquarium trade it is most often sold as Red Ammannia or Copper Leaf Ammannia, and the older spelling 'Ammania senegalensis' is still common in retail listings and care guides. It is sometimes confused with its larger relative Ammannia gracilis but is a distinct species, generally described as more compact in leaf size and habit — though under good conditions it still grows tall enough to serve as a background plant. Water parameters most widely cited place temperature around 22–28 °C in soft to moderately hard water (gH roughly 4–12 dGH), with pH most commonly given as 6.5–7.5, though some sources prefer a slightly tighter range of 6.5–7.0. To develop the characteristic pink-orange to copper-red coloration the plant is valued for, it generally needs high light (around 30–50 PAR over an 8–10 hour photoperiod), pressurized CO2 (roughly 25–30 mg/L), and consistent iron and potassium dosing alongside balanced macro fertilization. Under low light it tends to survive but stays green-yellow with weak color development. The best red coloration is reported when nitrate levels run lean while phosphate and micronutrients remain plentiful. The plant prefers a nutrient-rich substrate such as aquasoil or root tabs, and tends to be sensitive to abrupt parameter swings — sudden changes can trigger leaf melt, especially on freshly planted tissue-culture stems transitioning from above-water to submerged growth.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: cut the newest growth (about 5-10 cm with a few leaves) from a mature stem and replant in the substrate. Trimming the upper half of an established stem encourages bushier lateral side-shoot growth on the mother plant. When grown emersed, the plant produces small inconspicuous flowers from the leaf axils, but seed propagation is uncommon in the hobby.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for West African origin, care level (difficult), light (moderate to high), CO2 (recommended), growth rate (medium), placement (foreground/midground for shipped size), pH (6.5-7.5), propagation by cuttings, and coloration notes (brick red predominant, with green/orange/yellow accents). Buce Plant's listed family 'Onagraceae' is incorrect — corrected to Lythraceae based on Wikipedia and Aqua-Fish.net.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (71-83 °F / 21.7-28.3 °C), care level (moderate, intermediate-to-advanced), lighting (moderate to strong), CO2 (recommended), growth rate (fast under good conditions), background placement, and coloration (light green with copper tones through deep red/maroon/crimson).

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Lythraceae), native range (Senegal, Mali), adult size (30-45 cm), temperature (20-26 °C), pH (6.5-7.0), hardness (4-12 dGH), care level (medium-difficult), high lighting, CO2 strongly recommended, nutrient-rich substrate, iron and potassium dosing, and 10 cm stem-cutting propagation.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used to confirm taxonomy (Lythraceae, Myrtales) and native range (western Senegal, rice-paddy weed habitat), correcting Buce Plant's incorrect Onagraceae assignment.
